trending.title: EU Clears Prosus €4.1bn Just Eat Takeaway Acquisition — With Minority Stake Divestment Condition
trending.body: On 11 August 2025, the European Commission approved Prosus's (Naspers) €4.1 billion acquisition of Just Eat Takeaway.com (JET) under the EU Merger Regulation. Clearance was conditional: Prosus was required to reduce its 27.4% minority stake in iFood to below a threshold that would prevent the creation of a food delivery mega-merger spanning Brazil, Europe, and India. The Commission found that combined control of JET and a significant iFood position would raise horizontal and vertical competition...
trending.source: European Commission / Reuters / Global Competition Review
trending.prosus_lens: This is Prosus's own deal — the most significant M&A competition event in its recent history. The iFood stake-reduction condition sets a direct precedent: any future Prosus acquisition in food delivery will be scrutinised for portfolio-level overlaps across all Prosus ecosystems, not just the target market. The minority shareholding analysis methodology used by DG COMP is now a live template that will be applied to future Prosus deals involving iFood, Swiggy, or any overlapping portfolio company globally.

trending.title: EU Overhauling Merger Rules — 'European Champions' Policy Shift Opens Door for Larger Deals
trending.body: On 30 April 2026, the European Commission proposed a major overhaul of EU merger control rules in response to the Draghi report and Competitiveness Compass pressure to build European champions capable of competing with US and Chinese tech giants. For the first time globally, companies will be allowed to argue sustainability, resilience, investment, and innovation benefits to counter traditional consumer harm analysis. A new 'startup shield' will prevent Commission intervention in deals involving...
trending.prosus_lens: Directly relevant to Prosus's M&A strategy in Europe. The new 'innovation and investment benefits' defence is tailored for exactly the kind of deals Prosus executes — buying growth-stage platforms and scaling them with capital and ecosystem synergies. The startup shield (for non-gatekeeper acquirers) could simplify EU clearance for Prosus Ventures deals. The DMA gatekeeper carve-out does not affect Prosus (not designated). The 26 June consultation is an opportunity for Prosus to submit views on how the efficiencies framework should treat multi-sided platform mergers.

trending.title: Global Merger Control 2026: Pro-Business Shift in EU, UK & US — But New FSR and National Security Hurdles
trending.body: White & Case's April 2026 global M&A outlook identifies a fundamental shift toward more pro-business merger control across major jurisdictions — but warns of new non-competition hurdles that are increasingly deal-determinative. EU: revised merger guidelines expected by end 2027; Commissioner Ribera signalling innovation-focused reviews; Foreign Subsidies Regulation (FSR) now a 'key regulatory hurdle' for any deal with EU nexus where a party has received non-EU state subsidies above €50m. UK: CMA...
trending.prosus_lens: The FSR is the sleeper risk for Prosus M&A in Europe. As a Dutch-listed company with Tencent as a significant shareholder, any large EU acquisition by Prosus may face FSR scrutiny — requiring declaration of any state subsidies received by Tencent or other shareholders, and potential investigation by DG COMP. The UK's new 40-day pre-notification target and Phase I speed commitment directly benefits Prosus's JET integration and any future UK deals. In India, the ₹2,000 crore deal value threshold means almost every Prosus Ventures India investment now needs CCI assessment — including minority stakes where Prosus has 'material influence' over strategy.

trending.title: EU AI Act: Full Compliance Deadline 2 August 2026 — What Prosus Must Do Now
trending.body: The EU AI Act entered into force on 1 August 2024 and reaches its critical full-compliance deadline on 2 August 2026 — less than 3 months away. From that date, all high-risk AI systems must comply with conformity assessment, technical documentation, human oversight, and transparency obligations. GPAI (General Purpose AI) model obligations under Chapter V have already applied since 2 August 2025, meaning foundation model providers must already have codes of practice in place. Prohibited AI practi...
trending.prosus_lens: The 2 August 2026 full-compliance deadline is the single most urgent regulatory date for Prosus's AI portfolio. Ema (Universal AI Employee), Brainfish, and Advolve.AI all deploy AI agents in employment, enterprise, and customer-facing contexts — categories likely to trigger high-risk classification under Annex III. Prosus must complete conformity assessments, technical documentation, and human oversight frameworks across these products within weeks. GPAI model obligations already apply (since Aug 2025) — any Prosus portfolio company building on or deploying foundation models needs a codes-of-practice compliance posture today.

trending.title: South Africa Withdraws Draft AI Policy After 'Hallucinated' References Scandal — Reset Imminent
trending.body: On 26 April 2026, South Africa's Minister of Communications and Digital Technologies Solly Malatsi withdrew the Draft National AI Policy after it was exposed as containing fictitious and fabricated academic references — most plausibly the result of unverified AI-generated content used in the drafting process. The policy had proposed establishing a National AI Commission, an AI Ethics Board, and a new AI Regulatory Authority. Its withdrawal delays South Africa's AI governance framework indefinite...
trending.prosus_lens: South Africa is Naspers's home market and the base for Takealot, Property24, Autotrader, and Media24. The collapse of SA's AI policy process means the regulatory framework governing AI deployment in Prosus's South African portfolio remains the patchwork of POPIA, IP law, and sectoral rules — no consolidated AI governance for the foreseeable future. The institutional failure also has direct relevance to Tara's WSIS 2026 session on Agentic Fraud and Digital Trust: it is a live case study in what happens when AI governance institutions fail to govern their own use of AI.

trending.title: India 2026 AI Regulatory Stack: MeitY Advisory + DPDP Act + IT Rules — No Standalone AI Law
trending.body: India regulates AI not through a single law but through a layered multi-framework stack: (1) MeitY's March 2024 revised advisory requiring self-certification and AI content labelling for all AI platforms accessible to Indian users; (2) the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDP Act), with penalties up to ₹250 crore for non-compliance with data processing obligations — rules and the Data Protection Board are being operationalised through 2025-2026; (3) IT Intermediary Rules 2021 governin...
trending.prosus_lens: India is Prosus's single largest market by portfolio concentration — Swiggy, Meesho, PayU, PharmEasy, Rapido, and Brainly all operate AI systems at scale for Indian users. Every one of these platforms faces MeitY self-certification obligations, DPDP Act data processing duties, and the safe-harbour risk if their AI generates rather than hosts content. Swiggy's AI-powered recommendation and surge-pricing systems, Meesho's seller tools, and PayU's fraud detection are all in scope. The DPDP Act's 'right to erasure' for personal data embedded in model weights is a practical technical challenge Prosus's India tech teams must solve before the Data Protection Board becomes operational.

trending.title: Brazil AI Act (Bill 2338/2023): Chamber Vote Expected Before August 2026 Recess — Sanctions at 2% Revenue
trending.body: Brazil's AI Act (Senate Bill 2338/2023) was approved by the Federal Senate on 10 December 2024 and is now advancing through the Chamber of Deputies, where a special committee under Relator Deputy Aguinaldo Ribeiro was expected to vote by early May 2026. The bill adopts a risk-based approach mirroring the EU AI Act: high-risk AI systems face mandatory impact assessments, technical documentation, human oversight, and incident reporting; low-risk applications follow transparency guidance only. A Sy...
trending.prosus_lens: Brazil is Prosus's largest single revenue market through iFood, which dominates Brazilian food delivery with over 60% market share and deploys AI for dynamic pricing, driver dispatch, and restaurant recommendations — all potential 'high-risk' AI uses under the bill's risk tier. iFood's AI pricing systems could face mandatory impact assessments, human oversight requirements, and a public AI system registry obligation. OLX Brasil's AI-matching and fraud-detection systems are equally in scope. Creditas's credit-scoring AI is likely high-risk. With a Chamber vote potentially before August 2026 and a 180-day implementation clock, Prosus Brazil legal and product teams should be in compliance preparation now.

flash_alerts.title: EU Clears Prosus €4.1bn Just Eat Takeaway Acquisition — With Minority Stake Divestment Condition
flash_alerts.source: European Commission / Reuters / Global Competition Review
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: This is Prosus's own deal — the most significant M&A competition event in its recent history. The iFood stake-reduction condition sets a direct precedent: any future Prosus acquisition in food delivery will be scrutinised for portfolio-level overlaps across all Prosus ecosystems, not just the target market. The minority shareholding analysis methodology used by DG COMP is now a live template that will be applied to future Prosus deals involving iFood, Swiggy, or any overlapping portfolio company globally.

flash_alerts.title: India CCI Merger Control 2026: New ₹2,000 Crore Deal-Value Threshold Catches AI & Tech Acquisitions
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: Prosus Ventures is actively deploying capital in India. The new ₹2,000 crore deal value threshold means virtually every meaningful tech investment in India — including growth-stage rounds in Swiggy, Meesho, or new portfolio companies — must be assessed for CCI notification. The 'material influence' standard is particularly significant: Prosus regularly takes minority stakes with board representation, which CCI will now treat as notifiable control. Any future India AI acquisition (e.g. an AI agent company) will also face CCI's new AI market study scrutiny framework. Green Channel is available for clean deals — Prosus legal teams should pre-screen every India deal against SBOI and the overlap tests.

flash_alerts.title: Prosus Acquires Despegar — Latin America's Leading Online Travel Agency — Completing LatAm Ecosystem
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: Despegar deepens Prosus's LatAm exposure and creates a travel + food delivery + classifieds lifestyle ecosystem in Brazil — the kind of platform combination that DG COMP, CADE, and the new Brazil AI Act will each have views on. The AI pricing systems across Despegar and iFood in the same corporate group creates algorithmic collusion risk if any pricing coordination occurs — a live regulatory concern to monitor given CADE's Projeto Cérebro investigation into algorithmic pricing in Brazilian domestic markets.

flash_alerts.title: OLX Group Acquires La Centrale for €1.1 Billion — Transforming French Motors Classifieds Market
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: La Centrale establishes OLX in Western Europe for the first time and creates a platform that spans automotive classifieds from France to Romania to South Africa. Future OLX M&A in EU classifieds will now face scrutiny as horizontal consolidation — DG COMP will map the combined position across all OLX verticals (motors, property, general classifieds) in any overlap market. The DSA and DMA implications also grow: as OLX's European user base expands post La Centrale, VLOP threshold proximity increases, which would trigger systemic risk assessment obligations.

flash_alerts.title: EU Foreign Subsidies Regulation: Tencent Shareholding Creates Novel M&A Clearance Risk for Prosus
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: This is arguably Prosus's highest single M&A risk in Europe. Every EU acquisition above the FSR thresholds must be assessed for Tencent-related SFCs — a complex diligence exercise requiring Tencent cooperation and disclosure. The JET deal (2025) was the first major Prosus EU deal since FSR became operational; the approach taken there (and any DG COMP FSR findings) sets the precedent for all future Prosus EU M&A. Prosus legal and M&A teams should maintain a live FSR compliance protocol covering Tencent SFC disclosures updated annually.

flash_alerts.title: EU AI Act: Full Compliance Deadline 2 August 2026 — What Prosus Must Do Now
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: The 2 August 2026 full-compliance deadline is the single most urgent regulatory date for Prosus's AI portfolio. Ema (Universal AI Employee), Brainfish, and Advolve.AI all deploy AI agents in employment, enterprise, and customer-facing contexts — categories likely to trigger high-risk classification under Annex III. Prosus must complete conformity assessments, technical documentation, and human oversight frameworks across these products within weeks. GPAI model obligations already apply (since Aug 2025) — any Prosus portfolio company building on or deploying foundation models needs a codes-of-practice compliance posture today.

flash_alerts.title: South Africa Withdraws Draft AI Policy After 'Hallucinated' References Scandal — Reset Imminent
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: South Africa is Naspers's home market and the base for Takealot, Property24, Autotrader, and Media24. The collapse of SA's AI policy process means the regulatory framework governing AI deployment in Prosus's South African portfolio remains the patchwork of POPIA, IP law, and sectoral rules — no consolidated AI governance for the foreseeable future. The institutional failure also has direct relevance to Tara's WSIS 2026 session on Agentic Fraud and Digital Trust: it is a live case study in what happens when AI governance institutions fail to govern their own use of AI.

flash_alerts.title: India 2026 AI Regulatory Stack: MeitY Advisory + DPDP Act + IT Rules — No Standalone AI Law
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: India is Prosus's single largest market by portfolio concentration — Swiggy, Meesho, PayU, PharmEasy, Rapido, and Brainly all operate AI systems at scale for Indian users. Every one of these platforms faces MeitY self-certification obligations, DPDP Act data processing duties, and the safe-harbour risk if their AI generates rather than hosts content. Swiggy's AI-powered recommendation and surge-pricing systems, Meesho's seller tools, and PayU's fraud detection are all in scope. The DPDP Act's 'right to erasure' for personal data embedded in model weights is a practical technical challenge Prosus's India tech teams must solve before the Data Protection Board becomes operational.

flash_alerts.title: Brazil AI Act (Bill 2338/2023): Chamber Vote Expected Before August 2026 Recess — Sanctions at 2% Revenue
flash_alerts.prosus_lens: Brazil is Prosus's largest single revenue market through iFood, which dominates Brazilian food delivery with over 60% market share and deploys AI for dynamic pricing, driver dispatch, and restaurant recommendations — all potential 'high-risk' AI uses under the bill's risk tier. iFood's AI pricing systems could face mandatory impact assessments, human oversight requirements, and a public AI system registry obligation. OLX Brasil's AI-matching and fraud-detection systems are equally in scope. Creditas's credit-scoring AI is likely high-risk. With a Chamber vote potentially before August 2026 and a 180-day implementation clock, Prosus Brazil legal and product teams should be in compliance preparation now.

EU: revised merger guidelines expected by end 2027; Commissioner Ribera signalling innovation-focused reviews; Foreign Subsidies Regulation (FSR) now a 'key regulatory hurdle' for any deal with EU nexus where a party has received non-EU state subsidies above €50m. UK: CMA...
competition.prosus_lens: The FSR is the sleeper risk for Prosus M&A in Europe. As a Dutch-listed company with Tencent as a significant shareholder, any large EU acquisition by Prosus may face FSR scrutiny — requiring declaration of any state subsidies received by Tencent or other shareholders, and potential investigation by DG COMP. competition.title: India CCI Merger Control 2026: New ₹2,000 Crore Deal-Value Threshold Catches AI & Tech Acquisitions
competition.body: India's Competition (Amendment) Act 2023, fully operational in 2026, fundamentally changed merger control for tech deals. Key changes: (1) New deal value threshold of ₹2,000 crore (approx. €220m) — any acquisition in India where deal value exceeds this must be notified to CCI, even if the target has zero Indian assets or turnover, as long as it has 'substantial business operations in India' (SBOI); (2) 'Material influence' codified as the standard for control — minority stakes with board seats, ...
competition.prosus_lens: Prosus Ventures is actively deploying capital in India. The new ₹2,000 crore deal value threshold means virtually every meaningful tech investment in India — including growth-stage rounds in Swiggy, Meesho, or new portfolio companies — must be assessed for CCI notification. The 'material influence' standard is particularly significant: Prosus regularly takes minority stakes with board representation, which CCI will now treat as notifiable control. Any future India AI acquisition (e.g. an AI agent company) will also face CCI's new AI market study scrutiny framework. Green Channel is available for clean deals — Prosus legal teams should pre-screen every India deal against SBOI and the overlap tests.

competition.title: Prosus Acquires Despegar — Latin America's Leading Online Travel Agency — Completing LatAm Ecosystem
competition.body: In May 2025, Prosus completed its acquisition of 100% of Despegar, Latin America's leading online travel agency, announced December 2024 and recognised as LatinFinance's Private Equity Deal of the Year 2025. Despegar operates across 20+ Latin American markets, with primary presence in Brazil, Mexico, Argentina, Colombia, and Chile. The acquisition extends Prosus's LatAm ecosystem beyond iFood and OLX into travel and lifestyle. Despegar's AI-powered booking, recommendation, and dynamic pricing sy...
competition.prosus_lens: Despegar deepens Prosus's LatAm exposure and creates a travel + food delivery + classifieds lifestyle ecosystem in Brazil — the kind of platform combination that DG COMP, CADE, and the new Brazil AI Act will each have views on. The AI pricing systems across Despegar and iFood in the same corporate group creates algorithmic collusion risk if any pricing coordination occurs — a live regulatory concern to monitor given CADE's Projeto Cérebro investigation into algorithmic pricing in Brazilian domestic markets.

competition.title: OLX Group Acquires La Centrale for €1.1 Billion — Transforming French Motors Classifieds Market
competition.body: OLX Group (Prosus) acquired La Centrale, France's leading motors classifieds platform, for €1.1 billion, completing Prosus's consolidation of the European classifieds market. La Centrale competes directly with AutoScout24 and LaCentrale.fr is one of France's top automotive consumer destinations. The deal required EU merger clearance and French competition authority review given the horizontal overlap with OLX's existing European classifieds operations. The acquisition follows OLX Group's earlier...
competition.prosus_lens: La Centrale establishes OLX in Western Europe for the first time and creates a platform that spans automotive classifieds from France to Romania to South Africa. Future OLX M&A in EU classifieds will now face scrutiny as horizontal consolidation — DG COMP will map the combined position across all OLX verticals (motors, property, general classifieds) in any overlap market. The DSA and DMA implications also grow: as OLX's European user base expands post La Centrale, VLOP threshold proximity increases, which would trigger systemic risk assessment obligations.

competition.title: EU Foreign Subsidies Regulation: Tencent Shareholding Creates Novel M&A Clearance Risk for Prosus
competition.body: The EU Foreign Subsidies Regulation (FSR), in force since 2023 and fully cemented by 2025, requires mandatory notification of concentrations where a party has received non-EU state financial contributions (SFCs) above €50m in the three years prior to signing, and where the EU turnover and deal value thresholds are met. The key risk for Prosus: Tencent Holdings is a major Prosus shareholder. Any EU acquisition by Prosus may trigger DG COMP's FSR review alongside standard merger control, requiring...
competition.prosus_lens: This is arguably Prosus's highest single M&A risk in Europe. Every EU acquisition above the FSR thresholds must be assessed for Tencent-related SFCs — a complex diligence exercise requiring Tencent cooperation and disclosure. The JET deal (2025) was the first major Prosus EU deal since FSR became operational; the approach taken there (and any DG COMP FSR findings) sets the precedent for all future Prosus EU M&A. Prosus legal and M&A teams should maintain a live FSR compliance protocol covering Tencent SFC disclosures updated annually.

fintech.title: Paytm closes FY26 with net profit of Rs 552 crore
fintech.body: Tech & Internet Paytm closes FY26 with net profit of Rs 552 crore Digital payments major Paytm achieved its first full year of profit in FY26, reporting a net profit of Rs 552 crore. This turnaround was driven by cost control and significant revenue growth from financial services, with operating revenue rising 22% to Rs 8,437 crore.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Act: Full Compliance Deadline 2 August 2026 — What Prosus Must Do Now
ai_landscape.body: The EU AI Act entered into force on 1 August 2024 and reaches its critical full-compliance deadline on 2 August 2026 — less than 3 months away. From that date, all high-risk AI systems must comply with conformity assessment, technical documentation, human oversight, and transparency obligations. GPAI (General Purpose AI) model obligations under Chapter V have already applied since 2 August 2025, meaning foundation model providers must already have codes of practice in place. Prohibited AI practi...
ai_landscape.prosus_lens: The 2 August 2026 full-compliance deadline is the single most urgent regulatory date for Prosus's AI portfolio. Ema (Universal AI Employee), Brainfish, and Advolve.AI all deploy AI agents in employment, enterprise, and customer-facing contexts — categories likely to trigger high-risk classification under Annex III. Prosus must complete conformity assessments, technical documentation, and human oversight frameworks across these products within weeks. GPAI model obligations already apply (since Aug 2025) — any Prosus portfolio company building on or deploying foundation models needs a codes-of-practice compliance posture today.

ai_landscape.title: South Africa Withdraws Draft AI Policy After 'Hallucinated' References Scandal — Reset Imminent
ai_landscape.body: On 26 April 2026, South Africa's Minister of Communications and Digital Technologies Solly Malatsi withdrew the Draft National AI Policy after it was exposed as containing fictitious and fabricated academic references — most plausibly the result of unverified AI-generated content used in the drafting process. The policy had proposed establishing a National AI Commission, an AI Ethics Board, and a new AI Regulatory Authority. Its withdrawal delays South Africa's AI governance framework indefinite...
ai_landscape.prosus_lens: South Africa is Naspers's home market and the base for Takealot, Property24, Autotrader, and Media24. The collapse of SA's AI policy process means the regulatory framework governing AI deployment in Prosus's South African portfolio remains the patchwork of POPIA, IP law, and sectoral rules — no consolidated AI governance for the foreseeable future. The institutional failure also has direct relevance to Tara's WSIS 2026 session on Agentic Fraud and Digital Trust: it is a live case study in what happens when AI governance institutions fail to govern their own use of AI.

ai_landscape.title: India 2026 AI Regulatory Stack: MeitY Advisory + DPDP Act + IT Rules — No Standalone AI Law
ai_landscape.body: India regulates AI not through a single law but through a layered multi-framework stack: (1) MeitY's March 2024 revised advisory requiring self-certification and AI content labelling for all AI platforms accessible to Indian users; (2) the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDP Act), with penalties up to ₹250 crore for non-compliance with data processing obligations — rules and the Data Protection Board are being operationalised through 2025-2026; (3) IT Intermediary Rules 2021 governin...
ai_landscape.prosus_lens: India is Prosus's single largest market by portfolio concentration — Swiggy, Meesho, PayU, PharmEasy, Rapido, and Brainly all operate AI systems at scale for Indian users. Every one of these platforms faces MeitY self-certification obligations, DPDP Act data processing duties, and the safe-harbour risk if their AI generates rather than hosts content. Swiggy's AI-powered recommendation and surge-pricing systems, Meesho's seller tools, and PayU's fraud detection are all in scope. The DPDP Act's 'right to erasure' for personal data embedded in model weights is a practical technical challenge Prosus's India tech teams must solve before the Data Protection Board becomes operational.

ai_landscape.title: Brazil AI Act (Bill 2338/2023): Chamber Vote Expected Before August 2026 Recess — Sanctions at 2% Revenue
ai_landscape.body: Brazil's AI Act (Senate Bill 2338/2023) was approved by the Federal Senate on 10 December 2024 and is now advancing through the Chamber of Deputies, where a special committee under Relator Deputy Aguinaldo Ribeiro was expected to vote by early May 2026. The bill adopts a risk-based approach mirroring the EU AI Act: high-risk AI systems face mandatory impact assessments, technical documentation, human oversight, and incident reporting; low-risk applications follow transparency guidance only. A Sy...
ai_landscape.prosus_lens: Brazil is Prosus's largest single revenue market through iFood, which dominates Brazilian food delivery with over 60% market share and deploys AI for dynamic pricing, driver dispatch, and restaurant recommendations — all potential 'high-risk' AI uses under the bill's risk tier. iFood's AI pricing systems could face mandatory impact assessments, human oversight requirements, and a public AI system registry obligation. OLX Brasil's AI-matching and fraud-detection systems are equally in scope. Creditas's credit-scoring AI is likely high-risk. With a Chamber vote potentially before August 2026 and a 180-day implementation clock, Prosus Brazil legal and product teams should be in compliance preparation now.
